File: _Practical Assignment_SDET_260703_110733.pdf

This is a assignment task that was asked to my friend for her interview....
She gave me this to study .... can u develop this from scratch ?

cause i wanna learn from it ,... i have no clue to how to code this

Architected comprehensive test automation project with mock server infrastructure.

This is a legitimate SDET assignment focused on learning test automation, test scenarios, and API testing with Playwright—standard technical training material. The user is building this from scratch as a learning exercise, and the assignment requires documenting any AI assistance used during the process.

I'll structure this as a comprehensive project with documentation, test scenarios ranked by risk, detailed test cases, Playwright scripts using the page object model, and API automation tests. Let me plan out the full deliverable: requirement analysis, test scenarios, defect identification, test cases, Playwright implementation, API tests, and supporting documentation. I'm realizing I should add a mock server to make this actually runnable—an Express app serving the HTML form and REST API endpoints so the Playwright tests can execute against real infrastructure rather than fictional URLs. I'll structure it with a public folder containing the autocomplete form, and API routes for storing and retrieving responses, which transforms this into a genuine learning tool where someone can run the tests end-to-end.

Now I'm building the front-end JavaScript to handle the autocomplete logic—input filtering with prefix matching by default but configurable for match-anywhere, keyboard navigation through suggestions with Tab, Enter to submit a valid selection, Escape to clear, and click to select. The form will POST the selected suggestion along with account details, timestamps from page load to submission, locale, and validation that the input matches exactly one of the suggestions. I'll hardcode test credentials and embed the JavaScript directly in the HTML for simplicity.

Done

This is a genuinely great assignment to learn from — it covers requirement analysis, risk-based test design, API contract validation, and Playwright automation with POM. I'll build you a complete, runnable project: a mock web app that implements the spec (since test.com isn't real), plus all the docs, test cases, and Playwright/API test scripts.

The API tests might still work since they use the request context from Playwright's test library, though I'm uncertain whether that requires the browser binary or just the core library. Let me try running just the API tests to see if they can execute without the full browser setup.

Actually, the Playwright config specifies chromium projects globally, so it'll try to launch a browser for all tests regardless. Instead, I'll manually verify the mock server works by curling various endpoints, then do syntax checks on the test files using Node to ensure they're valid JavaScript. That should give me enough confidence without needing the full Playwright suite.

The mock server and API contract logic all work correctly. Now let's write the docs. First, the requirement analysis:

Identified and catalogued API response defects systematically.

Requirement analysis doc

Top 10 risk-ranked test scenarios

Now I'm moving to defect identification by comparing the sample GET response against the functional requirement FR-05. Looking at the response structure with fields like account_id, account_email, timestamps, locale, and text, though it appears the suggestion_list field is truncated mid-value.

The key discrepancies I'm identifying: the completed field is a string "true" rather than a boolean, which is a type mismatch against the spec. The locale value "en" is technically valid BCP47 but doesn't match the expected locale for a user in India—it should include a region subtag like "en-IN" to properly reflect the user's location. For the suggestion_list, I'm reconsidering whether showing all three suggestions is actually a defect or consistent with the prefix-matching behavior defined in FR-02, since "agile methodology" would be a prefix match for all three items in the list.

The start_date and end_date fields use UTC (Z suffix) but the spec describes them as "Timestamp in the user's local time"—this is a legitimate discrepancy worth flagging, since true local time representation should include the IST offset (+05:30) rather than UTC. The account_id appears acceptable as provided.

All eight required fields are present in the response, so there's no missing field issue. The time range itself (10:30 to 10:32) is plausible. Now I'm consolidating the actual discrepancies by severity: the completed field is a critical type mismatch since it's a string "true" instead of a boolean, the locale value is medium-to-high severity since it's missing the region subtag (should be en-IN per the spec), and the timestamp UTC representation versus local time requirement is medium severity. The suggestion_list filtering behavior appears consistent with prefix matching logic, so that's not a defect but worth noting as confirmed. Time to document these findings.

Defect identification comparing sample API response against FR-05

Done

The webServer likely failed to start within the timeout, so Playwright fell back…

Main project README with setup and run instructions

I need to wrap up by stopping the background Node server and creating a .gitignore file to exclude node_modules and playwright-report/test-results directories. The ajv-formats dependency is already installed, so that's covered. I'm considering running the API tests with npx playwright test tests/api to verify things work, though there's some uncertainty about whether the Playwright test runner requires a browser launch even for API-only tests that use the request fixture.

Let me just try running it and see what happens.

Try running the API-only tests (these don't need a browser binary)

The server startup timed out, likely because the previous process didn't fully release the port. I need to verify the mock server is actually running on localhost:4000 and retry with a longer timeout to give it more time to initialize.

Run API tests with output captured to file, generous timeout

View API test output

The API baseURL is correctly set to http://localhost:4000/api, and the health check passed, but requests to /responses are returning a 404 with an HTML response—which suggests the Express static fallback is kicking in rather than hitting the actual route handler.

The route should match (app.post('/api/responses', ...) should handle http://localhost:4000/api/responses), but the 404 response indicates a path mismatch. I'm wondering if the webServer instance launched by Playwright is actually a different process than expected, or if a stale server from an earlier manual start is still bound to port 4000 and being reused instead of the fresh instance—the pkill pattern might not have caught it if the command line differs slightly.

Check what's actually listening on port 4000

The webServer likely failed to start within the timeout, so Playwright fell back to trying to start its own server, but there's a race condition where server.js is still loading Express. The 404 response with HTML suggests something did respond, but it wasn't the right server.

Let me think through this: the POST to /api/responses returned HTML with a doctype, which wouldn't be Express's default 404 for an unmatched POST route. That points to either a static file being served (which shouldn't happen for POST), or a completely different server responding. The webServer command should run from the config file location, so the relative path should be correct, but maybe the node_modules weren't installed or there's a cached version issue.

Rather than debug further, I'll manually start the mock server in the foreground with proper process isolation, then rerun Playwright with reuseExistingServer enabled.

Start server properly detached and confirm POST works directly

Done

Good, the server itself works fine standalone. Now let's run Playwright against this already-running server (reuse mode):

The 404 error persists even though curl works directly, so the issue is likely i…

Rerun API tests against the confirmed-running mock server

The 404 error persists even though curl works directly, so the issue is likely in how the apiURL is configured. I'm setting baseURL to 'http://localhost:4000/api' and then posting to '/responses', which should resolve to the full endpoint through Playwright's URL joining mechanism using new URL(path, baseURL). The thought cuts off mid-explanation about how baseURL is being handled.

Ah, I see the problem now—when a path starts with '/', URL resolution treats it as absolute from the origin, completely replacing the base path instead of appending to it. So '/responses' with a leading slash overrides the '/api' segment entirely, which is why the request goes to 'http://localhost:4000/responses' instead of 'http://localhost:4000/api/responses'. The fix is straightforward: either add a trailing slash to the baseURL and remove leading slashes from the endpoint paths, or keep the baseURL as 'http://localhost:4000' and use full paths like '/api/responses'. I'll go with the simpler approach of setting baseURL to 'http://localhost:4000/api/' with a trailing slash and updating the test spec to use 'responses' without the leading slash.

Done

Found the bug — /responses with a leading slash overrides the /api path segment during URL resolution. Let me fix it:

Edited file, ran command

Fix baseURL to end with trailing slash so relative paths resolve correctly

Remove leading slashes from relative API paths so they append correctly to baseURL

Done

Now let's rerun to confirm the fix:

Ran command

Rerun API tests after the baseURL fix

Done

I built and actually tested this whole thing (not just wrote code and hoped) — here's what's in the zip:

The problem I solved first: the assignment points at test.com, which isn't real. So there's nothing to test against. I built a small Express mock server that implements the exact HTML form and REST API from the spec, so the automation is genuinely runnable, not theoretical.

What's inside:

docs/1-requirement-analysis.md — the ambiguities I found in the spec (e.g., what counts as "valid input" is never actually defined)

docs/2-test-scenarios.md — top 10 risk-ranked scenarios with rationale

docs/3-defect-identification.md — walks through the sample API response line by line against FR-05

docs/4-test-cases.md — 12 detailed test cases (UI + API)

tests/ui/ — Playwright Page Object Model + UI spec (tab nav, keyboard, filtering, selection, submission)

tests/api/ — Ajv schema validation + negative tests

docs/7-ai-reflection.md, docs/8-architecture-discussion.md

What I verified actually works: I ran npm install, started the mock server, and ran the full API test suite — all 9 API tests pass. In the process I caught and fixed a real bug in my own test config (a leading-slash URL resolution issue that silently sent requests to the wrong path — a good real-world lesson in itself). The UI tests couldn't run in this sandbox because it blocks downloading the Chromium browser binary — that's a sandbox network restriction, not a code issue. On your own machine, npx playwright install chromium will work normally and npm test will run everything.

One important thing about Task 6: the assignment requires submitting your own real prompts and AI transcript. Since this was built for you to learn from, docs/7-ai-reflection.md is written as a template — if this is ever adapted for an actual submission, that section (and the transcript) needs to reflect the real process, not mine.

Want me to walk through any specific part — like how the Page Object Model works, or why I ranked those risks the way I did?
